summary: Stuart Russell argues that the 'standard model' of AI—optimizing fixed, known objectives—is fundamentally flawed and dangerous as AI approaches general-purpose capabilities. He proposes a 'new model' based on assistance games, where AI systems are inherently uncertain about human preferences and must continuously learn them through observation, ensuring they remain deferential and controllable.

principles.statement: AI systems must be designed to be fundamentally uncertain about human objectives.
principles.rationale: If machines pursue fixed objectives that diverge from ours, it leads to catastrophic outcomes. Uncertainty about the true objective forces the machine to defer to humans, making it robust and controllable.
principles.source_quote: I propose a new model for AI development in which the machine’s uncertainty about the true objective leads to qualitatively new modes of behavior that are more robust, controllable, and deferential.

principles.statement: The standard model of AI is a mistake when applied to highly capable, real-world systems.
principles.rationale: The standard model assumes we can specify objectives completely and correctly. In complex real-world scenarios, this is impossible, and pursuing an imperfectly specified objective leads to a loss of control.
principles.source_quote: The standard model, then, despite all its achievements, is a mistake. The mistake comes from transferring a perfectly reasonable definition of intelligence from humans to machines.

principles.statement: AI success must be measured by real-world consequences, not just predictive accuracy.
principles.rationale: AI systems exist in a social context. Without a theory of sociotechnical embedding, relying purely on market forces or technical accuracy will fail to produce broadly beneficial outcomes.
principles.source_quote: Success is to be measured not by the accuracy of the AI system’s predictions and decisions, but by the real-world consequences of deploying the system.

frameworks.name: Assistance Games (Cooperative Inverse Reinforcement Learning)
frameworks.when_to_apply: When designing AI systems that need to act on behalf of humans without having a hard-coded, fixed objective.
steps: Formulate the machine's initial uncertainty about human preferences as a prior probability distribution.
Observe human behavior (choices, inaction, commands, requests, etc.) during interaction.
Update the probability distribution over the human reward function based on these observations, accounting for human cognitive limitations.
Take actions that maximize the inferred human reward while remaining deferential (e.g., allowing itself to be switched off) and minimally invasive.
frameworks.source_quote: In a Bayesian formulation, the robot begins with a prior probability distribution over the human reward function and updates it as the robot and human interact during the game.

mental_models.name: The Genie's Third Wish
mental_models.description: A metaphor for the danger of perfectly executing a poorly specified objective. When we ask a highly competent entity to fulfill a fixed objective, we inevitably leave out crucial constraints, requiring us to use our final wish to undo the damage.
mental_models.example: Used to explain why the standard model of AI (giving a machine a fixed objective) almost inevitably leads to a loss of human control.

mental_models.name: Sociotechnical Embedding
mental_models.description: Viewing AI deployment not just as a technical optimization problem, but as a system embedded in society, analogous to how city planning contextualizes the artifacts produced by civil engineering and architecture.
mental_models.example: Evaluating an AI system based on its real-world consequences and social externalities rather than just its predictive accuracy.

heuristics: A robot that is uncertain about the human’s preferences has a nonnegative incentive to allow itself to be switched off.
To avoid changing attributes of the world whose value is unknown, the robot will generally engage in 'minimally invasive' behavior.

signature_quotes.text: The standard model, then, despite all its achievements, is a mistake. The mistake comes from transferring a perfectly reasonable definition of intelligence from humans to machines.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the fundamental flaw in how AI has been defined since its inception.

signature_quotes.text: This is why, when a genie has granted us three wishes, our third wish is always to undo the first two wishes.
signature_quotes.context: Illustrating the difficulty of specifying human preferences completely and correctly.

signature_quotes.text: A more sensible definition of AI would have machines pursuing our objectives. Of course, our objectives... are in us, and not in the machines.
signature_quotes.context: Proposing the new model of AI based on uncertainty.

signature_quotes.text: Up to now, AI research has focused on systems that are better at making decisions, but this is not the same as making better decisions if human and machine objectives diverge.
signature_quotes.context: Concluding thoughts on the need to redefine AI.

anti_patterns.description: The Standard Model of AI (Fixed Objectives)
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: In the real world, humans cannot specify objectives completely and correctly. A highly capable machine pursuing a misaligned fixed objective will take preemptive steps to ensure achievement, leading to a loss of human control.

anti_patterns.description: Assuming perfect human rationality when learning preferences
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Humans are computationally limited and make mistakes. If an AI assumes perfect rationality, it might observe a human losing a game and incorrectly conclude the human preferred to lose. AI must account for actual human cognitive mechanisms.
